title: Bag of Words, TF-IDF, and Text Representation

questions.question: What does Bag of Words throw away?
questions.explanation: BoW counts tokens per document but discards their sequence.

questions.question: In the smoothed IDF formula log((N+1)/(df+1)) + 1, what does the trailing +1 ensure?
options: A word that appears in every document still has IDF 1 instead of 0
questions.explanation: The +1 keeps ubiquitous words at IDF=1 so they are not zeroed out, matching scikit-learn's default.

questions.question: Why L2-normalize TF-IDF rows before cosine similarity?
options: To convert sparse vectors to dense
Longer documents would otherwise dominate similarity scores; normalization puts all docs on the unit hypersphere
questions.explanation: L2 normalization removes document-length bias and turns cosine similarity into a dot product.

questions.question: Which TfidfVectorizer setting is risky to enable for sentiment analysis?
questions.explanation: English stopword lists drop negations like 'not', which carry sentiment signal.

questions.question: Which task does TF-IDF still win in 2026?
options: Spam detection, log anomaly flagging, and low-latency narrow classification
questions.explanation: TF-IDF beats embeddings when word presence is the signal and explainability or speed matter.

questions.question: Why does TF-IDF fail on the pair 'The movie was not good' vs 'The movie was excellent'?
options: Both documents share most tokens; bag-of-words has no notion of negation or word order
questions.explanation: Without word order or syntactic context, BoW cannot model that 'not' flips the sentiment of 'good'.
